original_abstract: The geometric structure and electronic configuration of reaction centers are the primary determinants of catalytic performance; however, these sites often undergo dynamic transformations under operating conditions that remain difficult to control. Here, we report a straightforward light-activation strategy to modulate the dynamic restructuring of Cu active sites on a TiO₂-supported catalyst during low-temperature methanol steam reforming. We show that under illumination, photoexcited charge carriers from the TiO₂ support trigger a structural restoration of the catalyst, reverting thermally deactivated Cu₂O back to its highly active metallic Cu state. This light-induced recovery results in a significant boost to both catalytic activity and long-term stability. Under low-intensity solar irradiation, the optimized Cu/TiO₂ catalyst achieves a H₂ production rate of 1724.1 μmol g⁻¹ min⁻¹, surpassing the performance of most traditional photocatalytic and thermocatalytic systems. By exploiting these strong light-matter-reactant interactions, we demonstrate the ability to manipulate Cu active sites in situ, offering a viable pathway for the real-time functionalization and maintenance of catalysts during operation.
humanized_abstract: The structure and electronic properties of reaction centers determine the performance of catalysts. The centers usually undergo unstable structural changes in the operational process, which is not easily controlled. This work reveals a method of readily activating light to regulate the unstable structural transformation of the active sites of catalysts (Cu species on the TiO2 support catalysts) in low temperature (150 °C) methanol steam reforming. The activity and stability of catalysts can be notably enhanced by up-shifting the structure of catalysts from thermally inactive Cu2O to their highly active metallic Cu states using charge carriers generated from the TiO2 support triggered by the light-activation. Under low light intensity, the optimized Cu/TiO2 catalyst yields a remarkably high rate of 1724.1 μmol g⁻¹ min⁻¹ H2 at least one order of magnitude higher than most of the reported photocatalysts and thermocatalysts. It reveals the strong coupling between light, matter and reactants, providing a convenient and timely strategy to modulate the active centers of the catalyst in situ, which is conducive to in-situ catalyst functionalization and maintenance.
